title: Statistics Basics — Bayesian Thinking, Bootstrap, and Power Analysis
key_insight: Frequentist statistics (p-values) answers: 'if H₀ is true, how unlikely is this data?' Bayesian statistics answers: 'given this data, what should I believe?' For small samples and prior knowledge, Bayesian is often more appropriate.
body:
## Bayesian Inference vs Frequentist

```python
# Frequentist: data is random, parameters are fixed
# p-value = P(data | H₀ is true)

# Bayesian: parameters are random (have distributions), data is fixed
# Posterior = P(parameters | data) ∝ P(data | parameters) × P(parameters)
#            = Likelihood × Prior

# Simple Bayesian coin flip example:
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Prior: we believe the coin is fair-ish (Beta distribution centered at 0.5)
# Observe: 7 heads out of 10 flips

heads = 7
tails = 3

# Prior: Beta(2, 2) — weak belief the coin is fair
prior_alpha, prior_beta = 2, 2

# Posterior: Beta(prior_alpha + heads, prior_beta + tails)
post_alpha = prior_alpha + heads
post_beta  = prior_beta + tails

p = np.linspace(0, 1, 300)
from scipy.stats import beta

plt.plot(p, beta.pdf(p, prior_alpha, prior_beta), label='Prior Beta(2,2)')
plt.plot(p, beta.pdf(p, post_alpha, post_beta),   label=f'Posterior Beta({post_alpha},{post_beta})')
plt.axvline(0.5, linestyle='--', color='grey', label='Fair coin')
plt.legend()
plt.title('Bayesian Coin Flip Update')
print(f'Posterior mean: {post_alpha/(post_alpha+post_beta):.3f}')  # 0.600
```

## Bootstrap: Non-Parametric Confidence Intervals

```python
import numpy as np

# Bootstrap: resample with replacement to estimate sampling distribution
# No distributional assumptions required — works when normality is violated

data = np.array([12.5, 15.3, 9.8, 14.2, 11.7, 16.1, 13.4, 10.9, 15.8, 12.1])
observed_mean = data.mean()

n_bootstrap = 10_000
bootstrap_means = np.array([
    np.random.choice(data, size=len(data), replace=True).mean()
    for _ in range(n_bootstrap)
])

# 95% confidence interval:
low, high = np.percentile(bootstrap_means, [2.5, 97.5])
print(f'Mean: {observed_mean:.3f}')
print(f'95% CI: [{low:.3f}, {high:.3f}]')

# Faster with numpy vectorisation:
resamples = np.random.choice(data, size=(n_bootstrap, len(data)), replace=True)
bootstrap_means = resamples.mean(axis=1)
low, high = np.percentile(bootstrap_means, [2.5, 97.5])
```

## Statistical Power and Sample Size

```python
from statsmodels.stats.power import TTestIndPower

# Power: probability of detecting an effect IF it truly exists
# Power = 1 - β (β = false negative rate)
# Standard target: 80% power (0.80)

# Effect size (Cohen's d): (μ₁ - μ₂) / pooled_std
# d = 0.2 small, 0.5 medium, 0.8 large

analysis = TTestIndPower()

# How many samples per group needed?
# Effect size: 0.5 (medium), alpha: 0.05, power: 0.80
n = analysis.solve_power(effect_size=0.5, alpha=0.05, power=0.80)
print(f'Required n per group: {n:.0f}')  # ~64

# What power do we have with n=30 per group?
power = analysis.solve_power(effect_size=0.5, alpha=0.05, nobs1=30)
print(f'Power with n=30: {power:.3f}')  # ~0.57 — only 57%! underpowered
```
common_mistakes: Stopping an A/B test as soon as p < 0.05 (peeking) — inflates false positive rate. Fix with sequential testing (O'Brien-Fleming bounds) or pre-specified sample size
Interpreting 95% CI as 'the true parameter is in this interval with 95% probability' — frequentist CI doesn't work that way. The Bayesian credible interval does have that interpretation
mini_exercise: You're running an A/B test. Expected effect size d=0.3 (small). What sample size do you need per group to achieve 80% power at α=0.05? Now compute: if you only got n=50 per group, what is your actual power? How does this affect your ability to detect the effect?
real_world_usage: Spotify uses Bayesian A/B testing with Thompson Sampling — instead of waiting for a fixed sample size, they continuously update beliefs about which variant is better and route more traffic to the winner. This reduces regret (showing a worse variant) while still maintaining statistical validity.
explain_back_prompt: What is the difference between a p-value and a Bayesian posterior? What is bootstrapping and when would you use it over a t-test? What is statistical power and why does it matter for experiment design?
